content:
“The Tool That Was Supposed to Replace You”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every generation of technology comes with a prediction. Someone declares the old way dead and the new thing inevitable. Sometimes they’re right about the change and wrong about what it means. Sometimes they’re right about both, and an entire category of work quietly disappears.
Web development has heard its death announced several times. No-code tools were going to make developers obsolete. Templates were going to make designers redundant. Now AI is going to do all of it while you sleep.
And yet.
The comparison that keeps coming back is the spreadsheet. When spreadsheets became widely available, people asked why anyone would still hire an accountant. The logic seemed airtight: the machine is faster, more accurate, and available to anyone with a computer. What’s left for the human to do?
What actually happened is that financial work didn’t go away — it got more complex. The people who understood numbers deeply used spreadsheets to do in an hour what used to take a week, then spent the rest of the time on the work that actually required judgment. The people who couldn’t read a balance sheet before spreadsheets couldn’t suddenly read one after them either.
AI is doing the same thing to web work.
Yes, AI can generate a layout. It can write a component, suggest a color palette, produce a block of functional code. What it cannot do is sit across from a business owner who isn’t sure what they need and ask the right questions until something useful emerges. It can’t weigh whether a feature that looks good in a demo will confuse real users six months from now. It can’t catch the moment when a technically correct decision is strategically the wrong one for this particular client in this particular market.
Those things require something AI doesn’t have: context, judgment, and the ability to care about the outcome.
The businesses that will feel this shift most are the ones chasing the cheapest version of everything. A $50 website built by an AI tool and a $50 website built by someone who didn’t know what they were doing look remarkably similar. They also fail in remarkably similar ways: unclear messaging, poor mobile experience, no real thought given to what the visitor is supposed to do when they arrive.
The bar for what “good” looks like has gone up, not down. Anyone can now produce something that looks like a website. Fewer people can produce one that actually works for the business behind it.
The tools will keep getting better. That’s not a threat — it’s an opportunity, but only for the people who understand the work well enough to use them well.
The question was never whether AI can write code or generate a design. The question is whether you know enough to tell the difference between the output that works and the one that doesn’t.
That knowledge doesn’t go out of style.
ByChaiStudio
